diagrams.title: Complete Android Project Directory Structure
diagrams.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the complete directory structure of a standard Android Studio project
elements: Subdirectories within app/: manifests/, java/, res/, build.gradle
Nested structure showing com.example.myapp package hierarchy
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to visualize the complete file organization of an Android project and understand the relationship between root-level and module-level configuration files

diagrams.title: App Module Internal Structure
diagrams.description: Detailed block diagram of the app/ directory showing all subdirectories and their contents
elements: java/ split into main/, androidTest/, test/ source sets
res/ showing layout/, drawable/, values/, menu/, mipmap/ subdirectories
Arrow relationships showing parent-child directory connections
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand where each type of file belongs within the app module and distinguishes between main source code, unit tests, and instrumented tests

diagrams.title: Resource Directory (res/) Structure
diagrams.description: Expanded view of the res/ directory showing all resource types and their purposes
elements: layout/ - XML layout files for UI
values/ - strings, colors, styles, themes, dimens
mipmap-hdpi/, mipmap-mdpi/, mipmap-xhdpi/, mipmap-xxhdpi/, mipmap-xxxhdpi/ - launcher icons
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to organize all visual and data resources properly and understand Android's resource qualifiers system for different screen densities

diagrams.title: Gradle Build Process Flow
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing how Gradle compiles and packages an Android application from source to APK
elements: Process 1: Gradle reads build.gradle (Project and Module level)
Process 2: AAPT (Android Asset Packaging Tool) compiles resources
Process 3: Java/Kotlin compiler converts source to bytecode
Process 4: DEX (Dalvik Executable) converter creates Dalvik bytecode
Process 5: APK Packager combines all files
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the build pipeline and how directory structure components are processed during app compilation

charts.title: Project Level vs Module Level build.gradle Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table: Project level (top-level) defines buildscript with repositories and dependencies, applies Android Gradle Plugin, configures global settings; Module level (app/) defines android{} block with compileSdk, buildToolsVersion, defaultConfig, buildTypes, dependencies{} block for app-specific libraries

charts.title: Source Sets in Android Project
charts.content: Three source sets: main/ (main application code and resources, always included), androidTest/ (instrumented tests running on device/emulator, located in java/com.example.app/androidTest/), test/ (unit tests running on local JVM, located in java/com.example.app/test/)

charts.title: AndroidManifest.xml Core Components
charts.content: Classification showing manifest elements: <manifest> root with package attribute; <uses-permission> for permissions; <application> with icon, label, theme attributes; <activity> elements with name, intent-filter for launcher; <service>, <receiver>, <provider> for components

charts.title: File Resolution Order in Gradle
charts.content: Flow: settings.gradle loaded first (defines modules) -> Root build.gradle executed (applies plugins, configures subprojects) -> App build.gradle executed (configures android extension, dependencies) -> Sources and resources compiled -> APK generated

branches: Root Level Configuration - build.gradle, settings.gradle, gradle/, gradle.properties
App Module (app/) - manifests/, java/, res/, build.gradle
Source Code (java/) - main/, androidTest/, test/ source sets
Resources (res/) - layout/, drawable/, values/, mipmap/, menu/, anim/
Build System - Gradle wrapper, plugin configuration, dependencies
Manifest - AndroidManifest.xml with permissions, activities, components
mindMaps.purpose: Provides a comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of Android project organization, helping students see how configuration files, source code, resources, and build systems interrelate to form a complete Android application project
